title: AI & the Digital Divide: Analyzing the Impact of Digital Connectivity on Human Development

indicators_measured.name: Proportion of individuals using the Internet
Proportion of population below international poverty line

indicators_targeted.name: Internet usage
indicators_targeted.rationale: Identifying causal factors of digital divide (47.1% Africa vs. 90.3% Americas internet access)

indicators_targeted.name: Broadband connectivity
indicators_targeted.rationale: Infrastructure quality shapes access to digital public services during COVID-19

indicators_targeted.name: Poverty rate
indicators_targeted.rationale: Connectivity exclusion compounds exclusion from public benefits and human development

non_undata_indicators_referenced.name: Human Development Index (HDI)
non_undata_indicators_referenced.note: Primary target variable in regression models; not found as a single undata DCID in the catalog

non_undata_indicators_referenced.name: E-Government Development Index (EGDI)
non_undata_indicators_referenced.note: Proxy for government digitalization; correlated with HDI in models

non_undata_indicators_referenced.name: Digital Accessibility Rights Evaluation (DARE) Index
non_undata_indicators_referenced.note: Disability digital accessibility score used to predict HDI and GDP

summary: UNDP commissioned an Omdena collaborative AI project to analyze how digital connectivity trends affect economic and human development globally, and to identify factors widening the digital divide—especially as COVID-19 shifted public services online. The team built a multi-source data dictionary and combined datasets on internet access, e-government (EGDI), gender metrics, education, age distribution, disability accessibility (G3ict DARE Index 2020), internet censorship, and technical restrictions. Methods include correlation analysis, country clustering, Support Vector Regression, decision trees, random forests, linear regression, principal component analysis, and an interactive Streamlit dashboard for real-time HDI prediction. Key findings: gender-specific features, internet censorship, technical restrictions, e-government maturity, and disability accessibility scores significantly associate with HDI. The project provides policy-relevant evidence for governments to address connectivity inequality and digital exclusion from public benefits.
